## Unit 5: Agriculture and Rural Land Use

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical Von Thünen scenario*

A small isolated city sits in the middle of a flat plain with uniform soil and one form of transportation. Land rents are highest closest to the city and fall with distance.

According to Von Thünen's model, which land use is most likely found in the ring closest to the city?

options.text: Extensive grain farming
Ranching
Market gardening and dairy
Forestry

questions.answer_headline: In Von Thünen's model, perishable, high-value goods like dairy and market gardening locate closest to the c...
questions.explanation_md: In Von Thünen's model, perishable, high-value goods like dairy and market gardening locate closest to the city because transport costs and spoilage matter most for them.

questions.question_text: Which of the following developments most directly challenges Von Thünen's original assumptions when applied to modern agriculture?

options.text: The fact that some cities are larger than others, producing differently sized market areas
Refrigerated transport and global supply chains, which weaken the link between distance from market and land use
The continued existence of subsistence farming in regions outside the market economy
The use of GPS-guided tractors and precision agriculture on commercial farms

questions.answer_headline: Refrigerated transport, global supply chains, and modern logistics weaken the link between physical distanc...
questions.explanation_md: Refrigerated transport, global supply chains, and modern logistics weaken the link between physical distance to market and the type of land use, which is one of Von Thünen's core assumptions.

questions.question_text: Thomas Malthus argued that:

options.text: Agricultural innovation would always keep pace with population growth, preventing any food shortage.
Population grows exponentially while food supply grows arithmetically, producing crisis unless population is checked.
Population pressure pushes societies to intensify agriculture and innovate their way out of scarcity.
Cities, by drawing labor away from farms, are the primary driver of national food shortages.

questions.answer_headline: Malthus argued that population grows geometrically (exponentially) while food supply grows arithmetically,...
questions.explanation_md: Malthus argued that population grows geometrically (exponentially) while food supply grows arithmetically, leading to crisis unless population is checked by famine, disease, or restraint.

questions.question_text: Ester Boserup's response to Malthus argued that:

options.text: Population growth, if left unchecked, necessarily leads to widespread famine and demographic collapse.
Food production sets a hard ceiling on population growth in every society and historical period.
Population pressure drives agricultural intensification and innovation, expanding food production to meet need.
Population always declines when resources become scarce, returning the system to equilibrium.

questions.answer_headline: Boserup pushed back on Malthus, arguing that population pressure spurs innovation and intensification in ag...
questions.explanation_md: Boserup pushed back on Malthus, arguing that population pressure spurs innovation and intensification in agriculture, expanding what the land can support.

questions.question_text: The Green Revolution of the mid-20th century is most directly associated with:

options.text: The original shift from hunting-gathering to settled agriculture during the Neolithic period
The spread of high-yield seed varieties, synthetic fertilizers, and irrigation across Asia and Latin America
The near-total collapse of subsistence agriculture across the developing world over recent decades
A widespread return to organic and small-scale farming methods within wealthier core economies

questions.answer_headline: The mid-20th-century Green Revolution introduced high-yield seed varieties (notably wheat and rice), synthe...
questions.explanation_md: The mid-20th-century Green Revolution introduced high-yield seed varieties (notably wheat and rice), synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation, especially in parts of South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Latin America.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical export composition*

| Country | Coffee | Bananas | Sugar | Manufactured Goods | Services |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Country V | 41% | 22% | 18% | 11% | 8% |
| Country W | 6% | 3% | 2% | 54% | 35% |

Which statement is best supported by the table?

options.text: Country V exhibits commodity dependency and is more vulnerable to global agricultural price swings.
Country W has a less diversified export economy than Country V and depends mainly on raw goods.
Both countries are equally exposed to commodity price shocks given their export composition.
Country V is in a later stage of economic development than Country W based on its export mix.

questions.answer_headline: Country V draws more than 80% of its exports from three primary commodities, which is the textbook profile...
questions.explanation_md: Country V draws more than 80% of its exports from three primary commodities, which is the textbook profile of commodity dependency. Price swings in coffee, bananas, or sugar will hit it hard, while Country W's diversified exports buffer it.

questions.question_text: The First (Neolithic) Agricultural Revolution is best characterized by:

options.text: The introduction of mechanized tractors and steel plows on European farms
The domestication of plants and animals and the shift toward settled farming
The spread of high-yield rice and wheat varieties across South and East Asia
The development of large-scale factory farming and confined animal feeding operations

questions.answer_headline: The First (Neolithic) Agricultural Revolution involved the domestication of plants and animals and the shif...
questions.explanation_md: The First (Neolithic) Agricultural Revolution involved the domestication of plants and animals and the shift toward settled farming, which gradually replaced hunting-gathering as the primary food strategy in many regions.

questions.question_text: The Second Agricultural Revolution, occurring alongside the Industrial Revolution, is most associated with:

options.text: The original domestication of the first crops in the Fertile Crescent and similar hearths
Improved farming techniques, crop rotation, and mechanization that raised yields and fed urban industrial workforces
The introduction of genetically modified organisms and biotech seeds in modern commercial agriculture
The collapse of European feudalism caused entirely by changing climate patterns in the Middle Ages

questions.answer_headline: The Second Agricultural Revolution accompanied the Industrial Revolution.
questions.explanation_md: The Second Agricultural Revolution accompanied the Industrial Revolution. Crop rotation, selective breeding, mechanization, and improved tools raised yields and freed labor for urban industrial work.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best distinguishes commercial agriculture from subsistence agriculture?

options.text: Commercial agriculture is always more environmentally sustainable than subsistence farming systems.
Commercial agriculture produces primarily for sale on the market, while subsistence farming feeds the producing household.
Subsistence agriculture relies on only the most modern machinery and high-input technology packages.
Commercial agriculture occurs only in periphery countries with low labor costs and weak land regulation.

questions.answer_headline: The defining difference is who the output is for: commercial agriculture sells into markets, while subsiste...
questions.explanation_md: The defining difference is who the output is for: commercial agriculture sells into markets, while subsistence agriculture feeds the producing household and community. The other contrasts in the answer choices are not reliable.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical land-rent scenario*

In a metropolitan region, land within 5 miles of the central market sells for $90,000 per acre. Land 30 miles from the market sells for $12,000 per acre. Land 80 miles from the market sells for $1,800 per acre.

A scale-of-analysis comparison of this region with a similar region across the country shows the same overall pattern, even though absolute prices differ. This is most consistent with:

options.text: The bid-rent theory and Von Thünen-style logic, where land rents fall with distance from market
A breakdown of classical locational theory in modern post-industrial economies
The Boserup hypothesis on agricultural intensification under population pressure
Wallerstein's world-systems theory of core, semi-periphery, and periphery

questions.answer_headline: Bid-rent and Von Thünen-style logic both predict that land use intensity and rents fall with distance from...
questions.explanation_md:
Bid-rent and Von Thünen-style logic both predict that land use intensity and rents fall with distance from market. Seeing the same pattern across regions at the same scale of analysis reinforces the theory's applicability. Topic 5.10. Skill 5.A.

## Unit 6: Cities and Urban Land Use

questions.question_text: The Burgess concentric zone model, developed using early-20th-century Chicago as a reference, describes urban form as:

options.text: Wedge-shaped sectors radiating outward from the CBD along major transportation corridors
A series of rings around a central business district, with land use changing ring by ring
Multiple specialized nodes scattered across the urban area, each anchoring its own activities
A spine of elite housing extending out from a colonial-era core, ringed by squatter settlements

questions.answer_headline: Burgess described urban form as concentric rings around a central business district, with each ring represe...
questions.explanation_md: Burgess described urban form as concentric rings around a central business district, with each ring representing a different land use and social profile, drawn from observations of early-20th-century Chicago.

questions.question_text: The Hoyt sector model differs from the Burgess model primarily because Hoyt argued that:

options.text: Cities have no central business district and grow outward from scattered residential nodes.
Urban land use develops in wedge-shaped sectors extending outward from the center, often along transport corridors.
All cities follow the same single concentric-ring pattern regardless of transportation.
Industry always locates at the geographic center of the city, displacing residential land use.

questions.answer_headline: Hoyt's sector model argued that urban land use forms wedge-shaped sectors radiating outward, often along tr...
questions.explanation_md: Hoyt's sector model argued that urban land use forms wedge-shaped sectors radiating outward, often along transport corridors, rather than uniform rings.

questions.question_text: The Harris-Ullman multiple nuclei model is best summarized as:

options.text: Cities organized around a single dominant central business district that drives all growth
Cities developing around several specialized nodes, each attracting compatible activities and repelling incompatible ones
Cities defined primarily by a colonial-era commercial spine and peripheral disamenity sectors
Cities in which the pattern of land use is determined entirely by the location of ethnic enclaves

questions.answer_headline: Harris and Ullman observed that real cities often grow around several specialized centers (industrial, reta...
questions.explanation_md: Harris and Ullman observed that real cities often grow around several specialized centers (industrial, retail, residential), with compatible activities clustering and incompatible ones repelling each other.

questions.question_text: The Griffin-Ford Latin American city model describes a city structure that includes:

options.text: Concentric rings of housing radiating outward from a single residential point, with no central business district at the core
A central CBD with a commercial spine of high-quality housing, ringed by zones of declining housing quality and disamenity sectors
Five evenly spaced sectors of identical land use radiating outward from a central colonial plaza at the city's geographic center
Multiple CBDs of equal size scattered across the metropolitan area, with no peripheral squatter settlements or disamenity zones

questions.answer_headline: The Griffin-Ford model of Latin American cities centers on a CBD with a commercial spine and elite resident...
questions.explanation_md: The Griffin-Ford model of Latin American cities centers on a CBD with a commercial spine and elite residential corridor extending outward, ringed by zones of progressively lower-quality housing and disamenity sectors at the periphery.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical urban hierarchy*

| City Rank | Population |
|---|---|
| 1st (Capital) | 12,000,000 |
| 2nd | 1,500,000 |
| 3rd | 1,100,000 |
| 4th | 850,000 |

According to the rank-size rule, the second-ranked city would be expected to have roughly 6 million people. The country's actual urban hierarchy is best described as exhibiting:

options.text: A rank-size distribution
A primate city pattern
Christaller's hexagonal lattice in equilibrium
A polycentric metropolitan structure

questions.answer_headline: A primate city is disproportionately larger than the next-largest city in its country (often more than twic...
questions.explanation_md:
A primate city is disproportionately larger than the next-largest city in its country (often more than twice as large). The 12 million versus 1.5 million gap is far above what the rank-size rule would predict, indicating primacy. Topic 6.3. Skill 1.B.

## Unit 7: Industrial and Economic Development

questions.question_text: Alfred Weber's least cost theory of industrial location argues that the optimal location for a manufacturing plant is determined by minimizing:

options.text: Labor costs alone, with no consideration of transport or material weight
Transportation costs, labor costs, and agglomeration effects together
Environmental impact and pollution control costs at the plant site
Land acquisition costs and prevailing trade tariffs at the regional border

questions.answer_headline: Weber's least cost theory treats optimal industrial location as the result of minimizing transportation cos...
questions.explanation_md: Weber's least cost theory treats optimal industrial location as the result of minimizing transportation costs, labor costs, and the pull of agglomeration (clustering with related firms).

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical employment by sector*

| Year | Agriculture | Manufacturing | Services |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1950 | 58% | 28% | 14% |
| 1985 | 21% | 41% | 38% |
| 2020 | 6% | 19% | 75% |

These employment shifts in Country Z are most consistent with:

options.text: The early stages of Rostow's traditional society, dominated by subsistence agriculture and barter trade
Movement through Rostow's stages toward high mass consumption, alongside post-industrial restructuring
A reversal of economic development as services come to replace stable manufacturing employment
Wallerstein's definition of a periphery economy locked into raw-material exports and dependent labor

questions.answer_headline: A long shift out of agriculture, through manufacturing, into services tracks Rostow's stages from tradition...
questions.explanation_md: A long shift out of agriculture, through manufacturing, into services tracks Rostow's stages from traditional society toward high mass consumption. The recent dominance of services also reflects post-industrial economic restructuring.

questions.question_text: Wallerstein's world-systems theory categorizes countries into core, semi-periphery, and periphery primarily on the basis of:

options.text: Population size and the country's total share of world inhabitants
Their position in global economic relationships, including production, trade, and capital flows
Climate, physical geography, and access to natural harbors
Dominant religious affiliation and shared cultural traditions

questions.answer_headline: Wallerstein's world-systems theory classifies countries as core, semi-periphery, or periphery based on thei...
questions.explanation_md: Wallerstein's world-systems theory classifies countries as core, semi-periphery, or periphery based on their role in global production, trade, and capital flows, not on climate, religion, or population alone.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical HDI components*

| Country | Life Expectancy at Birth | Mean Years of Schooling | GNI per capita (PPP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Country A | 82 | 13.1 | $48,000 |
| Country B | 67 | 6.4 | $7,200 |
| Country C | 75 | 9.8 | $18,000 |

Which statement is best supported by the data?

options.text: Country A is likely to have the highest HDI score because all three component indicators lead the table.
HDI rankings depend solely on GNI per capita and ignore health and education entirely.
Country B is more developed than Country A on the HDI based on its lower per-capita income.
The three indicators shown in the table are not used in standard HDI calculations.

questions.answer_headline: HDI combines life expectancy, education (mean and expected years of schooling), and GNI per capita.
questions.explanation_md: HDI combines life expectancy, education (mean and expected years of schooling), and GNI per capita. When a country leads on all three, its HDI score is highest.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical GII components*

| Country | Maternal Mortality (per 100,000 births) | Female Seats in Legislature | Female Labor Force Participation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Country D | 8 | 47% | 73% |
| Country E | 312 | 9% | 22% |

Which conclusion is best supported?

options.text: Country E likely has a higher (worse) GII score because all three indicators show substantially greater inequality.
The GII does not consider maternal mortality, only political and labor force participation.
Country D has more gender inequality than Country E given its share of legislative seats.
The two countries have effectively equivalent GII scores once the indicators are weighted.

questions.answer_headline: GII rises (worsens) with higher maternal mortality, lower female political representation, and lower female...
questions.explanation_md: GII rises (worsens) with higher maternal mortality, lower female political representation, and lower female labor force participation. Country E is far worse on all three, so its GII score will be substantially higher.

questions.question_text: The principle of comparative advantage suggests that countries benefit most from international trade when they:

options.text: Produce every good domestically that their citizens consume to maximize self-sufficiency
Specialize in goods for which they have a relatively lower opportunity cost and trade for the rest
Avoid trade with core economies and trade only within their regional bloc
Match the export profile of their single largest trading partner as closely as possible

questions.answer_headline: Comparative advantage means producing what you make at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for th...
questions.explanation_md: Comparative advantage means producing what you make at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for the rest. The point is relative efficiency, not absolute self-sufficiency or matching others' export profiles.

questions.question_text: A region's economy shifts from large factories with assembly-line production and long-term employment toward flexible production, contract labor, and service-sector jobs. This shift is best described as:

options.text: A return to Fordism, with stable mass production and lifetime factory employment
Economic restructuring toward a post-Fordist, service-oriented economy
A downward move from semi-periphery to periphery within Wallerstein's framework
Deglobalization driven by the retreat of multinational firms from the region

questions.answer_headline: The shift from large, vertically integrated factories with stable employment to flexible, contract-based, s...
questions.explanation_md: The shift from large, vertically integrated factories with stable employment to flexible, contract-based, service-heavy work is the textbook definition of post-Fordist restructuring.

questions.question_text: Sustainable development is most commonly defined as development that:

options.text: Maximizes GDP growth in the short term without regard to environmental or social factors
Meets present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own
Returns societies to pre-industrial economic patterns to lower their environmental footprint
Is led entirely by supranational organizations rather than by national or local actors

questions.answer_headline: The standard Brundtland-style definition of sustainable development is meeting present needs without compro...
questions.explanation_md: The standard Brundtland-style definition of sustainable development is meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.

questions.question_text:
*Stimulus — Hypothetical scale-of-analysis development data*

| Scale | HDI Score |
|---|---|
| Country J (national average) | 0.78 |
| Northern Region of J | 0.88 |
| Southern Region of J | 0.61 |
| Capital City within Southern Region | 0.83 |
| Rural District within Southern Region | 0.49 |

Which conclusion is best supported by the data?

options.text: Country J's single national HDI score accurately represents living conditions across the country.
Looking at development indicators only at the national scale can hide significant regional and local inequality.
The southern region is uniformly less developed than the northern region at every level of analysis.
Urban areas always show a lower HDI than rural areas because of overcrowding and pollution.

questions.answer_headline: A national HDI of 0.78 hides a high-development northern region, a much lower southern region, and even sha...
questions.explanation_md: A national HDI of 0.78 hides a high-development northern region, a much lower southern region, and even sharper urban-rural gaps within the south. National-scale data alone misses this, which is the scale-of-analysis lesson.
